mappings.rationaleAI: Linear algebra provides the mathematical framework for mechanics of particles (H.1), fluid mechanics (H.2), and electromagnetic theory (H.5); its vector and matrix methods are the core mathematical apparatus of these physical theories.
Artificial intelligence is explicitly listed as sub-topic G.6 of computer science; neural nets and probabilistic questions in AI also connect to automata theory concepts D.1 and D.2.

Statistics directly corresponds to section B, covering statistical inference (B.1), distribution functions including Gaussian distribution (B.1.b), estimation (B.2), hypothesis testing (B.3), and regression (B.4).

The computer article covers digital computation (A.7), digital calculators (A.7.a), programmed machines and digital computers (A.7.c), and computer architecture (G.3), spanning the history of computing machines — priority paths A.7.a and A.7.c.
Quantum mechanics is explicitly listed at H.8 (mathematical aspects of physical theories) and connects to statistical mechanics (H.4) through quantum statistical distributions foundational to both.

Computer science directly maps to section G in full: algorithms (G.1), theory of computation (G.2), computer architecture (G.3), programming languages (G.4), numeric computation (G.5), and artificial intelligence (G.6).

Probability is the mathematical foundation of statistical inference (B.1) and provides the framework for probabilistic questions in automata theory (D.3); its axiomatic formulation directly supports both sections.

Covers numeral systems and numerical notations (A.1), ancient numerical notations across cultures including Hindu-Arabic (A.1.b), and the development of decimal notation (A.1.c) — addressing three priority paths directly.
Gauss's method of least squares is foundational to statistical estimation (B.2), and his contributions to differential geometry underlie Riemannian geometry (H.7) as applied in physical theories.

Classical mechanics directly corresponds to mechanics of particles and systems (H.1) and mechanics of solids (H.3) — both priority paths — and also covers fluid dynamics (H.2) through Lagrangian and Hamiltonian frameworks.
Algorithms are the subject of synthesis and analysis (G.1) and theory of computation (G.2); they also connect to programmed machines (A.7.c) as the instructions executed by digital computers.
Functions underlie graphs and graphical procedures (A.5.b) and are the central constructs of programming languages and methodology (G.4) — both priority paths.
Equations are central to numerical analysis — solution of linear, nonlinear, and differential equations (C.4) — and connect to algebraic notation in calculatory algebra (A.4.a).
Geometry covers early applications (A.2.a), instruments for observation and navigation (A.2.b), and applications to celestial measurement (A.2.d) as geometric aids in applied mathematics.

Euler made decisive contributions to mechanics of particles and systems (H.1), fluid dynamics (H.2), and logarithms as calculatory tools (A.4.b); his mathematical physics work spans much of section H.

Newton's Principia established classical mechanics (H.1), and his calculus and gravitational theory are foundational to the mathematical treatment of physical theories including relativity's predecessor framework (H.6).

Einstein's general relativity directly corresponds to relativity theory and space-time as a four-dimensional continuum (H.6), requires Riemannian geometry (H.7), and his Brownian motion/Bose-Einstein statistics work connects to statistical mechanics (H.4).

Turing founded theory of computation (G.2) via Turing machines, established automata theory concepts (D.1, D.4 — priority paths), and pioneered artificial intelligence (G.6) through the Turing test and early stored-program computing.
Cryptography is explicitly listed as an application of information theory (F.4) and connects to theory of computation (G.2) through computational hardness assumptions underlying public-key schemes.
Measurement connects to units used to assist counting and grouping (A.1.a — priority path) and to mathematical tables used in practical standardized calculation (A.5.a — priority path).

Robotics applies automata theory (D.1 — priority path — physical analogy to automata), control theory (E.4), and artificial intelligence (G.6); the field's theoretical basis in automata and control is directly relevant.
Force is the central concept of mechanics of particles and systems (H.1 — priority path) and mechanics of solids (H.3 — priority path); electromagnetic force connects to electromagnetic theory (H.5).
Space as a mathematical concept connects to relativity theory and spacetime (H.6) and to Riemannian geometry (H.7), which provides the mathematical framework for curved space in general relativity.
Fractions are part of decimal notation and modern notational developments (A.1.c — priority path) and relevant to algebraic notation in calculatory algebra (A.4.a).
Arithmetic as the elementary calculatory science underlies section A broadly, including numerical notations (A.1), decimal notation (A.1.c — priority path), and calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4).

Covers ancient numerical notations (A.1.b — priority), development of decimal notation (A.1.c — priority), early geometric applications (A.2), and history of logarithms (A.4.b) — addressing multiple priority paths directly.

Al-Khwarizmi introduced the decimal positional number system (A.1.c — priority), established algebraic notation and calculation methods (A.4.a), and his work on computation directly precedes logarithms as calculatory tools (A.4.b).

Physics as a discipline encompasses all the physical theories in section H — mechanics (H.1, H.3 — priority paths), fluid mechanics (H.2), statistical mechanics (H.4 — priority), electromagnetism (H.5), relativity (H.6), and quantum mechanics (H.8).
Astronomy connects to the application of geometry to celestial measurement (A.2.d) and to instruments for observation and navigation (A.2.b) as historical drivers of mathematical tool development.
Electricity, unified with magnetism through Maxwell's equations, corresponds to the mathematical aspects of electromagnetic theory (H.5).
Electromagnetism governed by Maxwell's equations is the primary subject of H.5 (electromagnetic theory) as a mathematical aspect of physical theories.
Complex numbers are mathematically essential in electromagnetic theory (H.5 via phasors and wave equations) and quantum mechanics (H.8 via wave functions and Hilbert space formalism).
Three-dimensional Euclidean space is the arena in which mechanics of particles (H.1 — priority), fluid mechanics (H.2), and mechanics of solids (H.3 — priority) are formulated and solved.
Gravity is central to mechanics of particles (H.1 — priority path via Newtonian gravitation) and to relativity theory (H.6 via Einstein's interpretation of gravity as spacetime curvature).
Momentum is the central conserved quantity in mechanics of particles (H.1 — priority), generalized in fluid mechanics (H.2), and distributed statistically in statistical mechanics (H.4 — priority).
Exponentiation connects to logarithms as calculatory tools (A.4.b) and to cryptographic applications (F.4) through modular exponentiation underlying RSA and related schemes.
Physical cosmology applies relativity theory and the mathematical treatment of spacetime (H.6) as its primary theoretical framework for modeling the universe's large-scale structure.

Time as a physical dimension connects to relativity theory and space-time as a four-dimensional continuum (H.6) and appears as the fundamental variable in mechanics of particles and systems (H.1 — priority).

Mathematical analysis provides the underpinning for numerical analysis (C — approximation, error, solution of equations) and the mathematical apparatus for physical theories (H) including differential equations and functional analysis.
Number theory underlies cryptographic applications of information theory (F.4 — RSA and related systems) and primality-testing algorithms (G.1 synthesis and analysis of algorithms).
